description: Code coverage measures what portion of your source code is executed during testing, helping identify untested paths. Apply it to set minimum coverage standards in CI/CD and prioritize testing efforts on critical business logic. This methodology delivers visibility into gaps and guides testing focus, though high coverage alone doesn't guarantee quality—aim for 80%+ on critical paths rather than chasing 100% on trivial code.

Decomposition patterns provide concrete techniques for breaking large code units into manageable pieces: extract service (move logic from views), extract component (split UI into focused parts), extract module (group related functionality), extract configuration (separate environment-specific settings), and extract types (consolidate definitions). Use these patterns when files exceed size limits or when multiple people edit the same file, reducing conflicts and improving readability.

Code decomposition principles guide when and how to split files based on single responsibility, optimal file size (50-200 lines for most types), and LLM-friendly context budgeting. Apply the checklist: split when lines exceed 300, multiple classes exist, or responsibilities mix; don't split tightly coupled code or small units under 100 lines. This delivers 40-60% complexity reduction in core codebase and enables parallel development.

Code quality trends 2026 consolidates current best practices including TypeScript 5 strict mode, React 19 server components, Python 3.12+ type hints, and 81% team adoption of AI coding tools. Reference this to align your stack with production-ready standards: TypeScript strict, React 19, Next.js 15, Python 3.12+, 80%+ test coverage, and Lighthouse >90.

Code review is systematic examination of source code to find bugs, improve quality, ensure consistency, and share knowledge. This methodology is split into fundamentals (basics.md covering principles and checklists) and process (process.md covering templates and scenarios). Apply reviews to all code changes before merging, especially security-sensitive and architectural changes, delivering 20-30% reduction in production defects.

Code review basics covers what to examine (correctness, design, maintainability, testing, performance, security), how to write constructive comments (distinguish blocking bugs from suggestions and nitpicks), and how to respond promptly. Use this to establish review standards that balance thoroughness with velocity—review within 4 hours, distinguish comment types clearly, and approve when good enough rather than perfect.

Code review process provides PR templates, automated checks, and concrete review scenarios (bugs, design issues, security problems, N+1 queries). Implement this to standardize review workflow: keep PRs under 400 lines, provide clear descriptions, use CI checks for formatting/linting, track metrics like time-to-review and rework rate. This delivery speeds feedback cycles and catches issues before production.

Documentation creation (CLAUDE.md style) establishes AI-readable context files of 100-150 lines per directory, covering purpose, structure, key concepts, entry points, and common operations. Create CLAUDE.md for every module with source code, using progressive detail and self-contained sections. This methodology delivers clarity for LLM-assisted development, enables faster onboarding, and serves as a single source of truth for architectural context.

Mob programming is all-hands-on-deck development where the whole team works on one thing together with driver (keyboard), navigators (direction), and optional facilitator (timer/focus). Apply this to complex features, critical bugs, and team learning—rotate driver every 5-15 minutes with clear communication. This delivers superior code quality, distributed knowledge, eliminates silos, and reduces onboarding time despite perceived efficiency loss.

Pair programming pairs two developers at one workstation: driver types while navigator reviews and thinks strategically, switching roles frequently. Use it for complex/unfamiliar code, knowledge transfer, critical logic, and difficult debugging—skip it for routine tasks or when deep focus is required. This methodology reduces bugs, spreads knowledge, and prevents single points of failure, with studies showing net positive productivity.

Refactoring patterns (extract method/function, extract class, replace conditional with polymorphism, introduce parameter object, replace magic numbers, decompose conditional) improve code readability and maintainability without changing behavior. Apply incrementally in small steps with tests green throughout; use IDE refactoring tools for safety. This delivers cleaner code that's easier to maintain and extend.

Technical debt represents the cost of choosing quick solutions over better approaches, tracked in debt registers with type (code/design/test/infrastructure), severity, location, and estimated fix effort. Manage debt explicitly: classify by Martin Fowler's quadrant (reckless/prudent, deliberate/inadvertent), measure complexity and coverage, and budget time for paydown. This methodology prevents debt accumulation and maintains feature velocity.

Bun is a high-performance JavaScript runtime with built-in tooling (bundler, transpiler, test runner, package manager) and native APIs (password hashing, WebSocket, file operations). Use Bun for new TypeScript-first projects needing maximum performance; stick with Node.js for legacy code and maximum compatibility. This runtime delivers 1.5-3x speed improvements over Node.js.

React decomposition patterns establish LLM-friendly structure with components under 150 lines, separate hooks (40-60 lines), and feature-based organization rather than layer-based. Break god components into presentational components, custom hooks for state, and services for API calls. This approach improves readability, testability, and reusability while keeping files manageable for AI-assisted development.

Modern JavaScript/TypeScript 2025-2026 establishes core patterns: TypeScript strict mode, prefer const and arrow functions, named exports over default, explicit return types for public APIs. Use Node.js 22 LTS or Bun for runtimes; React 19 or Next.js 15 for frontend; Express 5 or Fastify 5 for backend. This stack delivers type safety, better tooling, and performance improvements.

JavaScript testing with Vitest and Jest covers unit tests (isolated fast tests), component testing (React Testing Library), mocking (vi.mock), and API testing (MSW). Structure tests with describe/it blocks, focus on user behavior not implementation, use type-safe mocks. Aim for 80%+ coverage on critical paths, test edge cases and error conditions, and keep test files maintainable.

Express is a mature, ecosystem-rich Node.js framework for REST APIs with composable middleware, centralized error handling, and graceful shutdown. Use Express when ecosystem compatibility matters or for teams familiar with middleware patterns; structure with routers, validation middleware, auth, and error handlers. This framework offers maximum flexibility and community support with moderate learning curve.

Fastify is a high-performance Node.js framework with plugin architecture, built-in schema validation (TypeBox/JSON Schema), type safety, and native async support. Use Fastify for performance-critical APIs or TypeScript-first projects; it runs 2-3x faster than Express in benchmarks. This framework delivers automatic OpenAPI docs, cleaner code, and better DevX.

Node.js patterns establish backend structure: controllers (HTTP handlers), services (business logic), middleware (auth, logging, validation), models (entities), and error handlers. Apply dependency injection, async/await, and proper error handling. Use Pino for structured logging, Zod for validation, custom error classes for context, and graceful shutdown hooks.

React hooks enable functional components with state, side effects, and custom logic extraction. Master useState (with lazy initialization), useEffect (with cleanup and dependencies), useCallback/useMemo (for memoization), useRef (for persistent values), useReducer (for complex state), and Context (for global state). Follow the rules of hooks strictly, manage dependencies carefully, and avoid common pitfalls like stale closures.

React patterns guide component architecture (feature-based directory structure, reusable UI primitives), functional components with explicit props typing, custom hooks for stateful logic, Context for cross-component state, and performance optimization (memo, useMemo, useCallback). Decide state management based on: server state (TanStack Query), form state (React Hook Form), global UI state (Zustand), or local state (useState).

TypeScript patterns enable advanced type safety: utility types (Partial, Pick, Omit), generics with constraints, type guards, discriminated unions, assertion functions, Zod schema validation, and branded types. Use template literal types for type-safe strings, conditional types for transformations, and const assertions for literal inference. This delivers compile-time safety and prevents runtime errors.

TypeScript & React 2026 patterns establish modern stack: TypeScript 5.x strict mode, React 19 with Server Components, Next.js 15 App Router. Key practices: server components for data fetching (zero JS), minimal client boundaries, Server Actions for mutations, type-safe props, and streaming for performance. This architecture delivers 38% faster initial loads.

TypeScript strict mode enables all type safety checks: noImplicitAny, strictNullChecks, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, exactOptionalPropertyTypes. Master null handling with optional chaining, type narrowing with guards, branded types for primitive safety, and const assertions for literal types. Use unknown over any, avoid non-null assertions, and validate at runtime. This delivers production-ready type safety.

Django REST API development compares Django REST Framework (DRF 3.15+, mature, 1000+ packages, best for enterprise) with Django Ninja (1.x, modern, fast, best for greenfield projects). Choose DRF for complex permissions and large teams; choose Ninja for performance and modern Python. Implement ViewSets/routers for CRUD, APIViews for custom endpoints, JWT authentication with 15-30 min access tokens, cursor pagination for large datasets, and drf-spectacular for OpenAPI docs.

The BaseModel pattern provides a consistent foundation for all Django models with UUID and timestamp fields, preventing ID enumeration attacks and enabling audit trails. Use this when starting any Django project or refactoring existing models to eliminate duplicate code across all model classes. It delivers security, maintainability, and a single source of truth for common behaviors across your data layer.

A comprehensive decision framework that guides you through Django architecture choices: framework selection, API framework choice, database selection, and deployment strategy. Use this when starting a new project or evaluating architectural alternatives to avoid costly refactoring later. It delivers clarity on trade-offs and confidence in your technical decisions.

This guide solves circular import problems and import organization chaos through cross-app aliases, TYPE_CHECKING guards, and lazy imports. Apply it to prevent runtime errors, improve import clarity, and enable IDE autocomplete across your codebase. It delivers import statements that clearly show dependency direction and zero circular dependencies.

A comprehensive guide to modern Django model design covering BaseModel patterns, soft delete, custom managers, QuerySets, and field choices using TextChoices. Use this when designing models to ensure they remain thin, database-correct, and performant through the lifecycle of your project. It delivers models that are safe to refactor, efficient to query, and maintainable.

A reference covering pytest-django with Factory Boy, model_bakery, Django REST Framework testing, and coverage optimization. Use when building test suites to distinguish between pytest and unittest, understand factory patterns, and integrate testing into your workflow. It delivers comprehensive test coverage that catches real bugs.

Modern asyncio patterns including TaskGroup, Semaphore, timeout context managers, and async best practices for Python 3.11+. Apply this when building I/O-bound applications, APIs, or concurrent operations to unlock performance without threading complexity. It delivers applications that handle thousands of concurrent connections efficiently.

Comprehensive foundation covering data structures, control flow, functions, OOP, error handling, and modern tooling with uv and Ruff. Use when learning Python or onboarding developers to establish shared understanding of modern practices and idioms. It delivers productive Python developers who follow current best practices.

Modern Python code quality using Ruff (10-100x faster than Black+flake8), mypy/pyright for type checking, and pre-commit hooks for CI/CD. Apply it to your project setup to catch style, type, and quality issues before code review, dramatically reducing feedback cycles. It delivers consistent, high-quality code with minimal friction.

Modern FastAPI patterns for production async APIs with Pydantic v2 validation, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async integration, and dependency injection. Use when building high-performance REST APIs to leverage automatic OpenAPI docs, type safety, and the fastest Python web framework. It delivers APIs that are fast, well-documented, and typesafe.

Python 3.12+ features including PEP 695 generics, improved f-strings, free-threading (3.13+), and modern tooling with uv and Ruff. Apply this when starting new projects to use the latest language features, unlock performance improvements, and avoid technical debt. It delivers future-proof code that runs 15% faster with better type safety.

The Python ecosystem landscape covering frameworks, domains, tooling trends, and modern practices for 2025-2026. Use this as a reference to understand where Python fits in your technology decisions and what tools are current in the ecosystem. It delivers informed decisions on framework and tool selection.

Poetry 2.x setup with PEP 621 compliance, dependency management, and lock file best practices. Apply this when initializing new projects to ensure reproducible builds, deterministic dependency resolution, and easy team collaboration. It delivers consistent development and production environments.

Complete guide to Python type hints from basics through advanced patterns like Protocols, TypeVar, and TypeIs for modern type safety. Apply this to add type hints to your codebase, enabling better IDE support, documentation, and early error detection through type checkers. It delivers self-documenting code that catches bugs before runtime.

Deep dive into Python's typing system covering TypedDict, Protocol, generic syntax, and framework-specific patterns for Django and FastAPI. Use this reference when implementing complex type structures to ensure type safety across your application layers. It delivers type-safe code that scales with your codebase.

Comparison of Django, FastAPI, and Flask covering architecture, performance, features, and decision criteria for each framework. Use this to select the right framework for your project based on requirements like async support, admin panels, or API performance. It delivers technology decisions aligned with your actual needs.

Testing strategies for APIs including contract testing with Pact, integration testing, OpenAPI validation, and Postman collections. Apply this when building APIs to ensure contracts between consumer and provider, validate responses against specs, and catch integration bugs early. It delivers reliable APIs that meet consumer expectations.

Latest software development patterns for AI-assisted coding, TypeScript 5.x strict modes, React 19 Server Components, Python 3.13, and AI testing tools. Use this as a current reference for 2026 best practices in AI tools, type safety, and testing to stay current with the industry. It delivers modern, production-grade code using latest tooling.

Bun runtime fundamentals covering project setup, native HTTP serving, Hono framework integration, file operations, and testing. Use this when building TypeScript projects that prioritize startup speed and development experience with all-in-one tooling. It delivers fast development cycles with 10-100x faster package installation.

Comprehensive coverage guide covering coverage types, tools, configuration, CI/CD integration, and the philosophy that coverage is a guide not a goal. Apply this to identify untested code paths and set quality gates without chasing 100% that tests trivial code. It delivers focused testing that covers what matters.

Code review process templates, scenarios, and metrics for effective feedback on bugs, design, security, and performance issues. Use this to establish code review practices that catch problems before deployment while maintaining positive team dynamics. It delivers higher code quality through collaborative improvement.

CSS-in-JS fundamentals with styled-components and Emotion for component-scoped styling, theming, and dynamic props-based styles. Apply this when building component libraries to achieve scoped styles without class name conflicts and seamless theming support. It delivers maintainable, scalable styling without global namespace pollution.

Django REST Framework API patterns provide thin, maintainable views that delegate business logic to services while using OpenAPI documentation for clarity. Apply when building REST endpoints to separate HTTP concerns from business logic and ensure consistent API documentation across your project. This delivers cleaner code organization, easier testing of business logic, and auto-generated API documentation that stays in sync.

The Base Model pattern establishes a consistent foundation for all Django models with UUID, timestamps, and soft-delete support, ensuring data integrity and audit trails across your application. Use this for any new Django project or when refactoring legacy models to improve consistency and avoid duplicating common fields. The value includes automatic tracking of all changes, secure external identifiers via UUIDs, and reduced migration friction.

Django coding standards enforce consistent project structure, import conventions, and service-layer patterns that make code predictable and maintainable across large teams. Apply these when starting a new Django project or onboarding new developers to establish team-wide expectations for code organization. The outcome is reduced cognitive load during code reviews, faster onboarding, and fewer architectural debates.

A decision tree for code placement eliminates ambiguity about whether functions belong in services, utils, models, or views based on their responsibilities and side effects. Use this during feature development and code reviews to ensure consistent architecture without lengthy architectural discussions. This delivers predictability—any team member can find code in seconds and implement features in the right layer the first time.

Django import patterns with aliases prevent circular dependencies and naming conflicts while maintaining clean, readable code across apps. Apply this convention when starting a new project and enforce it in code reviews to avoid the subtle bugs that come from poor import organization. The benefit is eliminating common import-related errors and making the dependency graph of your codebase explicit and navigable.

Django model patterns cover field design, ForeignKey strategies, indexing, and Django 5.0+ features for building efficient, maintainable database schemas. Use this reference when designing models for new features to avoid N+1 queries, index inefficiencies, and accidental data loss from CASCADE deletes. The value includes better query performance, fewer migration headaches, and data safety through proper relationship handling.

pytest fixtures and parametrization provide a powerful, readable approach to testing Django applications that's faster than the built-in TestCase class. Apply this when writing unit tests for services, integration tests for APIs, and factory-based test data generation to avoid brittle test setups. This delivers faster test suites, clearer test intent, and easier test maintenance as your codebase evolves.

Django quality tools (Black, isort, flake8, mypy) enforce consistent formatting and catch bugs early through pre-commit hooks. Use this in every Django project with ruff as the primary tool to avoid manual formatting debates and catch type errors before runtime. The outcome is cleaner diffs, fewer linting discussions in code review, and fewer silent bugs from type mismatches.

Testing with pytest offers factories (model_bakery, factory_boy) and parametrization for creating realistic test data efficiently. Use this when writing integration tests for API endpoints and database operations where you need real data fixtures. The benefit is eliminating test maintenance burden from hardcoded mock data and making tests readable to team members unfamiliar with the codebase.

CLAUDE.md documentation follows a structured, AI-readable format that provides context for any directory without overwhelming readers with detail. Create one for every module folder when starting a new project or onboarding new developers to make the codebase self-documenting. This delivers reduced onboarding time, better codebase navigation, and clearer understanding of architectural intent.

E2E tests with Playwright validate complete user workflows through the entire application stack using Page Object Models for maintainability. Apply selectively to critical user journeys (signup, checkout, payment) rather than testing everything to avoid slow, brittle suites. The value is catching integration issues that unit and integration tests miss while staying fast enough to run regularly.

Standardized error responses using RFC 7807 Problem Details provide consistent, actionable error information to clients with proper HTTP status codes and trace IDs. Use this pattern in all APIs to make errors debuggable and ensure clients can handle them programmatically. This improves developer experience, enables better monitoring, and reduces support burden from unclear error messages.

A comprehensive index of all files in a knowledge domain helps navigators find content by purpose, language, or type without memorizing the structure. Maintain this when documenting large knowledge bases to enable quick access to specific patterns or techniques. The benefit is reducing cognitive load when searching for methodology details and making knowledge portable across teams.

Go backend patterns using Gin or Echo provide clean routing, middleware composition, and error handling for building performant REST APIs and microservices. Use when building Go APIs to standardize project layout and HTTP patterns across your team. This delivers clean, testable code organization and makes Go projects approachable for developers from other languages.

Goroutine patterns, worker pools, and context-based cancellation provide safe concurrent programming without data races or goroutine leaks. Apply when building high-performance services that need to process multiple requests or tasks simultaneously without overwhelming resources. The outcome is predictable concurrency behavior, easy resource management, and systems that scale without crashing.

Custom error types with HTTP status codes and error codes enable consistent error handling across Go services. Use this pattern in production Go services to provide meaningful errors to clients and make application behavior testable. This improves debugging, enables proper HTTP status code handling, and makes error recovery strategies explicit.

HTTP handler patterns for Gin and Echo provide consistent routing, middleware chains, and request validation. Apply when building Go APIs to standardize how HTTP concerns are handled and make handler code focused and testable. This delivers readable routing configurations, composable middleware, and handlers that are easy to test in isolation.

Integration tests using real databases (Testcontainers), APIs (Supertest), and service mocks (WireMock) verify component interactions work correctly end-to-end. Use these tests for database interactions, API endpoints with authentication, and external service integrations where mocks would hide real problems. The benefit is catching integration issues early while maintaining reasonable test speed.

JavaScript/TypeScript standards with strict mode, explicit types, and modern syntax patterns provide safety and clarity in JavaScript development. Apply from the start of any new JavaScript project to prevent common errors and make refactoring safer. This delivers fewer runtime errors, better IDE support, and code that's easier to understand months later.

A selection matrix for languages and frameworks helps teams choose the right tool for each component based on requirements and strengths. Use when starting new services or migrating legacy components to make intentional technology decisions rather than defaulting to what you know. The value is picking tools that fit the actual problem and avoiding over-engineering with powerful but inappropriate frameworks.

An index of all development methodologies organized by problem type enables quick discovery of relevant patterns and practices. Maintain and update this regularly as you discover new patterns to make methodology knowledge discoverable across your organization. This ensures best practices are applied consistently and new team members learn from documented solutions rather than reinventing approaches.

Mob programming with rotation, clear roles, and agreement-based decisions leverages group intelligence for complex problems and knowledge sharing. Use for critical features, complex bugs no one can solve alone, and team learning to accelerate decision-making and distribute knowledge. The outcome is higher-quality solutions, faster onboarding for new team members, and stronger team alignment.

Mobile-first CSS with fluid layouts, responsive images, and touch-friendly interactions ensures optimal user experience across all devices. Apply to every web project from the start—designing mobile first prevents later retrofitting and keeps stylesheets simpler. This delivers better mobile performance, improved accessibility, and reduced maintenance burden from device-specific workarounds.

Mocking strategies distinguish between stubs (state), spies (behavior verification), fakes (working implementations), and mocks (expectations) to isolate tests properly. Use the right double for each test—stubs for state verification, fakes for complex scenarios, mocks sparingly to verify critical interactions. This delivers fast, focused tests that don't break on refactoring and clearly communicate intent.

Express and Fastify patterns provide application structure, middleware composition, validation, and error handling for building production Node.js APIs. Use Express for maximum ecosystem compatibility or Fastify for better performance and TypeScript support when building REST APIs or microservices. The value is cleaner routing organization, consistent error handling, and applications that are testable and maintainable.

Pair programming brings two developers to one workstation, where driver and navigator roles rotate to maintain focus and catch errors in real-time. It excels for complex code, knowledge transfer, and critical logic, reducing defects by up to 15% with minimal slowdown. Use it selectively on challenging tasks rather than routine work, alternating roles every 25-30 minutes and maintaining high-quality communication throughout the session.

pnpm is a fast, disk-efficient package manager using content-addressable storage and symlinks, enforcing strict dependency isolation by default. It prevents phantom dependencies that npm allows and provides superior workspace support for monorepos. Commit pnpm-lock.yaml for reproducible builds, use strict-peer-dependencies=true to catch version conflicts early, and leverage workspaces for managing multiple packages in a single repository.

Python is a versatile language enabling rapid development for backend services, data pipelines, and automation. Use type hints with modern syntax (Python 3.9+), follow PEP 8 conventions, and leverage the rich ecosystem of frameworks like Django and FastAPI. Consider Python for I/O-bound operations and scripting, but be aware of the GIL limitation for CPU-bound parallelism; async/await patterns optimize performance for network-bound tasks.

FastAPI combines asynchronous support, automatic OpenAPI documentation, and Pydantic validation to build production-grade APIs with minimal boilerplate. Use dependency injection via Depends() for testability, define routers by domain, and separate business logic into service layers. The framework shines for microservices and high-throughput APIs where async/await prevents blocking I/O.

Poetry simplifies Python project setup with deterministic lock files, workspace monorepo support, and dependency groups for separating dev/test/docs requirements. Always commit poetry.lock for reproducible environments across machines and CI/CD pipelines. Use dependency groups to keep production images slim, configure virtual environments in-project for easier debugging, and leverage Poetry's publishing tools for PyPI distribution.

Type hints document intent and enable static analysis with mypy to catch bugs before runtime without changing runtime behavior. Use modern syntax (str | None instead of Optional), prefer specific types over Any, and leverage Protocol for duck typing when inheritance is overkill. Enable strict mode in mypy to enforce complete coverage, use TypedDict for structured dicts, and apply generic types for reusable patterns.

React hooks enable state and side-effect management in functional components, replacing class-based patterns with simpler composition. Use useState for state, useEffect for subscriptions with proper cleanup, and useCallback/useMemo judiciously to optimize only performance bottlenecks. Custom hooks extract logic for reuse; follow the Rules of Hooks strictly and depend on all used values to prevent stale closures.

Refactoring improves code structure without changing behavior by extracting methods, removing duplication, and renaming for clarity. Always keep tests green to catch regressions, work in small incremental steps, and use IDE refactoring tools rather than manual edits. Refactor before adding features to reduce friction; common patterns include Extract Method, Extract Class, and Replace Conditional with Polymorphism.

Rust uses Result and Option types for explicit error handling instead of exceptions, preventing unexpected panics in production. Use the ? operator for ergonomic error propagation, define custom error types with thiserror crate, and leverage pattern matching for exhaustive handling. Never unwrap in production code; prefer Result with clear error context for APIs and validate inputs early to fail fast.

Rust's ownership system enables memory safety without garbage collection by tracking who owns each value at compile time. Understand the three rules: each value has one owner, ownership transfers on assignment, and references enable borrowing. Smart pointers like Arc and Rc provide shared ownership for specific scenarios; use Cow for copy-on-write optimization and RAII ensures resources cleanup.

Rust's test framework lives in the same file as production code under #[cfg(test)] modules with mockall for mocking complex dependencies. Use table-driven tests with parametrization, leverage tokio::test for async testing, and separate unit tests (fast, isolated) from integration tests (real dependencies). Mock only external boundaries; test behavior, not implementation details.

shadcn/ui provides copy-paste component primitives built on headless Radix UI with Tailwind styling, not npm packages. Extend components via composition rather than modifying ui/ files, use class-variance-authority (CVA) for variant management, and ensure semantic HTML and ARIA attributes for accessibility. Theme with CSS variables, customize Tailwind config for your design system.

Storybook isolates component development from application context, enabling visual testing and design system documentation. Write stories demonstrating use cases with args for interactivity, use Chromatic for visual regression detection, and leverage interaction testing with userEvent to catch behavioral issues. Organize by feature hierarchy (Components/Buttons/Button) and keep stories up-to-date with component changes.

Tailwind's utility-first approach enables rapid UI development with JIT compilation producing minimal CSS. Use design tokens in tailwind.config.js rather than hardcoded values, apply concentric CSS ordering for readability, and extract repeated patterns into components instead of @apply. Mobile-first responsive design with md: and lg: prefixes; leverage the cn() helper for conditional class merging.

Test-Driven Development writes failing tests first, then minimal code to pass, then refactors while tests stay green. This Red-Green-Refactor cycle ensures code meets requirements and is testable by design. Use TDD for complex logic, API design, and bug fixes; it documents expected behavior through tests and enables safe refactoring with confidence.

Fixtures provide consistent test data and setup, reducing duplication and improving readability compared to inline setup. Use pytest's fixture system with appropriate scopes (function for isolation, session for expensive setup), leverage factory patterns for flexible data generation, and ensure cleanup via yield for resource management. Fixtures compose—higher-level fixtures depend on lower-level ones.

Testing validates behavior across unit, integration, and E2E levels; unit tests catch logic errors fast, integration tests verify component interaction, and E2E tests validate user journeys. Follow the Test Pyramid (70% unit, 20% integration, 10% E2E) to balance speed and confidence. Mock external dependencies in unit tests, use real or containerized services for integration, and test critical workflows end-to-end.

TypeScript strict mode enables comprehensive type checking preventing common errors like null/undefined access and implicit any. Enable strict in tsconfig.json, use unknown instead of any for type-safe assertions, and leverage type narrowing with guards and discriminated unions. Branded types prevent mixing similar primitives, const assertions enable literal types, and template literal types create domain-specific type patterns.

Unit tests verify individual components in isolation with fast execution and high coverage to catch regressions early. Follow Arrange-Act-Assert structure, use parametrization to test multiple scenarios efficiently, and keep tests independent so they run in any order. Mock external dependencies, test behavior not implementation, and avoid brittle tests that break on refactoring without behavior change.

End-to-end testing simulates real user workflows through the entire application stack from UI to database, catching integration issues unit tests miss. Use Playwright for modern E2E testing with built-in mobile emulation and multi-browser support, Page Object Model for maintainable selectors, and mock external APIs while keeping your application and database real. Run only critical user journeys (10% of test pyramid) to keep suite fast.

Integration testing verifies multiple components work together with real or containerized dependencies, catching configuration and database interaction errors. Use Testcontainers to spin up ephemeral PostgreSQL/Redis instances, transaction rollback for test isolation and speed, and factory fixtures for consistent test data. Balance between transaction rollback (fast, local) and database reset (accurate, catches constraints) based on your needs.

Mocking isolates code under test from slow, unreliable, or expensive external dependencies like APIs and databases. Mock at system boundaries, never internal logic; use test doubles taxonomy (stubs for responses, mocks for behavior verification, fakes for working implementations). Keep mocks simple, prefer interfaces for dependency injection, and avoid over-mocking which decouples tests from reality.

Test-Driven Development cycles through Red (failing test), Green (minimal code to pass), and Refactor (improve without breaking tests) to ensure testability and requirement alignment. Write tests before code to design clean APIs, use TDD for bug fixes by first reproducing the issue in a test, and benefit from comprehensive test coverage enabling fearless refactoring throughout the codebase.

Fixtures provide reusable test setup and data, reducing duplication and improving clarity across test suites using factory patterns, builders, and parametrization. Use fixture composition where higher-level fixtures depend on lower-level ones, apply appropriate scopes (function for fresh data, session for expensive resources), and ensure cleanup via yield statements. Fake implementations work better than mocks for complex scenarios.

Go's built-in testing package enables unit tests, benchmarks, and fuzzing without external frameworks, with table-driven tests and subtests as idiomatic patterns. Define interfaces at the consumer for dependency injection and mocking, use httptest for HTTP handler testing, and leverage testcontainers-go for integration tests with Docker. Run tests with -race flag to detect concurrency issues and -cover for coverage reporting.

Choose between Vitest (10-20x faster, native ESM, TypeScript) for modern Vite projects and Jest (mandatory for React Native, legacy CommonJS codebases). Use the test pyramid with 70% unit tests, 20% integration, 10% E2E. Set up MSW for API mocking and Playwright for cross-browser testing. This framework comparison and setup guide helps you pick the right tool for your JavaScript/TypeScript project and avoid lock-in regrets.

Master core testing patterns—AAA (Arrange-Act-Assert), Given-When-Then (BDD), Test Data Builder, Page Object Model—that make tests readable and maintainable across teams. Learn when to use mocks vs stubs, how to structure tests to prevent flaky failures, and why the test pyramid (70/20/10 ratio) beats the ice-cream-cone anti-pattern. These patterns apply to Python, JavaScript, Go, and other languages, saving time and preventing test rewrites.

pytest 8.x brings improved assertion diffs, better async support (pytest-asyncio), and 1000+ plugins for parallel testing, coverage, mocking, and more. Master fixtures (dependency injection with function/class/module/session scopes), parametrization (run same test with multiple inputs), and markers (tag and filter tests). LangSmith integration and configuration in pyproject.toml make pytest the default for modern Python testing.

Apply FIRST principles (Fast, Isolated, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely) to write unit tests that verify individual components in isolation. Use AAA (Arrange-Act-Assert) structure, follow one assertion per concept, and avoid testing implementation details. Know when to mock (external APIs, databases) vs test behavior (domain logic). Aim for 80%+ coverage on public APIs and business logic; skip trivial getters and framework-generated code.

Build production-ready autonomous agents with memory (short-term and long-term recall via embeddings), tool systems (function calling with error handling), and planning capabilities (max iterations, timeout protection). Implement memory management (summarization, relevance filtering), safety guardrails (human approval, sandboxed execution), and monitoring (action logging, token tracking). This modular framework prevents infinite loops, handles errors gracefully, and scales from simple ReAct loops to complex multi-iteration planning.

Implement four core agent patterns—ReAct (Reason→Act→Observe loop for general tasks), Plan-and-Execute (upfront planning with dependency tracking), Reflexion (self-critique and retry), and AutoGPT-style (full autonomy with goals)—to handle different problem complexity levels. ReAct works best for web search and Q&A; Plan-and-Execute excels at multi-step projects; Reflexion learns from mistakes; AutoGPT handles research and automation. Code examples show how to wire tools, manage state, and handle timeouts.

Move beyond simple prompt→response with agent design patterns: Chain of Thought (step-by-step reasoning), ReAct (reasoning + tool use), Plan-and-Execute (plan first then act), and Tool Use (function calling). Compare frameworks—LangGraph (state machines, complex flows), AutoGen (multi-agent conversations), CrewAI (role-based teams), OpenAI Agents SDK (official support). These patterns unlock complex reasoning tasks that single-pass prompts cannot solve.

Implement AI governance frameworks addressing risk classification (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal), transparency requirements (disclose AI nature, emotion recognition use), human oversight, bias detection, and data governance. Use model cards (Hugging Face format), bias detection tools (Fairlearn, AI Fairness 360), explainability methods (SHAP, LIME), and monitoring systems. This ensures compliance with regulations, builds user trust, and prevents costly recalls or legal exposure.

Autonomous agents combine reasoning, memory, and action capabilities to accomplish complex objectives with minimal human intervention. Choose from ReAct (Reason+Act loop), Plan-and-Execute (upfront planning), Reflexion (self-improvement), or AutoGPT-style (full autonomy). Manage memory (short-term, long-term, recall), define tools with clear descriptions, set iteration limits, and implement error recovery. This reference guides when to use agents vs simpler chains and how to monitor production deployments.

August 2026 marks full enforcement of the EU AI Act: high-risk systems require quality management, risk assessment, technical documentation, conformity proof, EU database registration, and human oversight. Risk classification determines obligations—social scoring prohibited, biometrics/employment AI highly regulated, chatbots require transparency. Penalties up to 35M EUR or 7% global revenue. Checklist includes model cards, bias detection, explainability tools, incident reporting, and training data documentation.

Build ReAct agents (think step-by-step, use tools, observe results), Plan-and-Execute agents (decompose complex tasks first), and LATS (Language Agent Tree Search with backtracking) using LangChain and LangGraph. Define tools with @tool decorator or StructuredTool with JSON schemas, bind them to models, and handle tool errors gracefully. These architectures handle tool orchestration, multi-step planning, and complex reasoning better than single LLM calls.

Coordinate multiple AI agents—Supervisor (one routes to specialists), Debate (reach consensus through argument), Hierarchical (nested teams with delegation)—using LangGraph state machines. Test agents via mock LLM calls, verify output format, and run end-to-end integration tests. Multi-agent patterns solve problems requiring diverse expertise, verification through debate, and role-based task delegation more effectively than monolithic single-agent systems.

LangChain provides abstractions for prompts, chains, memory, and retrieval to build LLM apps rapidly. Use LCEL (LangChain Expression Language) pipe syntax (prompt|model|parser) for composable, streamable chains. Choose from sequential, router, MapReduce, or fallback patterns depending on control flow. Connect to many LLMs and tools, cache responses, and trace with LangSmith. Start here before agents if you need basic chains and prompt templates.

Compose LLM chains using LCEL pipe syntax: prompt templates → LLM → output parsers. Implement sequential (A→B→C), router (dynamic branching), MapReduce (parallel + reduce), and fallback (resilience) patterns. Use prompt templates with few-shot learning, system messages, and message history placeholders. Parse outputs as JSON, Pydantic, or strings. Streaming yields chunks as they arrive; error handling via retries and fallbacks prevents silent failures.

Store conversation history using four memory types—Buffer (full history for short chats), Summary (compress old messages, scalable), Vector (semantic search over past interactions), Entity (track named entities). Use Conversation Summary Memory for long-form chats; auto-merge retriever for hierarchical chunks. LangGraph workflows use TypedDict state with Annotated fields for accumulation. Human-in-the-loop checkpoints pause execution for approval; subgraphs enable modular composition.

LangGraph enables stateful workflows with complex control flow. Define state as TypedDict with Annotated fields (operators like add for accumulation), create node functions (pure, return partial state updates), add conditional edges (route based on state), and compile with checkpointers for persistence. Human-in-the-loop patterns pause at approval nodes; subgraphs enable modular nesting; streaming yields state updates; error handlers route on failures. This replaces simple chains when workflows need branching, loops, or human interaction.

Build ReAct agents (reasoning + tool use) and OpenAI agents (function calling) over knowledge bases. Evaluate retrieval (MRR, hit rate), response quality (faithfulness, relevancy), and correctness with LLM-based evaluators. Compare agents pairwise. Implement production patterns—caching embeddings, streaming responses, async queries, error handling with retries. LlamaParse (managed parsing) and LlamaCloud (managed indexing) reduce ops overhead. Monitor with callbacks and Langfuse integration.

Load documents from files, web, APIs (1500+ connectors via LlamaHub), parse into nodes with semantic chunking or hierarchical grouping, extract metadata (title, summary, keywords), and embed. LlamaIndex specializes in RAG pipelines over knowledge bases; LangChain handles orchestration and agents. Chunk size choice matters—256-512 tokens for precise retrieval, 1024+ for context. Semantic splitting uses embeddings to break on topic boundaries, hierarchical parsing creates parent-child node relationships.

Choose index type based on query pattern: VectorStoreIndex (semantic search, primary), KeywordTableIndex (BM25 for keywords), KnowledgeGraphIndex (entities and relationships), TreeIndex (hierarchical summaries). Query engines retrieve context and synthesize responses. Response modes—refine (iterative, accurate), compact (fast, single call), tree_summarize (large context). Retrievers support hybrid (BM25+vector), reranking, auto-merging. SubQuestionQueryEngine decomposes complex questions; RouterQueryEngine routes to specialized indices.

MCP (Model Context Protocol) standardizes how LLMs connect to tools and data sources. Anthropic-developed, tool definitions are reusable across clients. Solves the fragmentation problem where each LLM had proprietary tool formats. Security through protocol design, composable capabilities, standard definitions. MCP servers expose tools; MCP clients (Claude Code, etc.) invoke them. Benefits: reusability, security, composability, cross-client standardization. Foundation for the next-generation LLM tooling ecosystem.

Coordinate multiple specialized agents to solve complex problems. Patterns—Sequential (pipeline), Parallel (fan-out/gather), Hierarchical (manager delegates), Debate (agents argue positions), Collaborative (iterate together). Implement agents with consistent system prompts, orchestrators routing messages, and conversation logging. Sequential pipeline transforms output through stages; debate system reaches consensus; hierarchical manager decomposes tasks. Test coordination logic, handle deadlocks, monitor inter-agent communication. Multi-agent systems tackle problems beyond single-agent scope.

Enterprise multi-agent architectures use eight design patterns—Sequential Pipeline, Parallel Fan-Out/Gather, Hierarchical Decomposition, Generator+Critic, Loop (iterative refinement), Human-in-Loop, Router, Blackboard (shared workspace). Framework comparison: CrewAI (low setup, role-based), AutoGen (multi-agent conversations), LangGraph (state machines), AgentFlow (low-code canvas), NVIDIA Nemotron (high throughput). Enterprise adoption accelerating—40% of apps will have AI agents by 2026 (Gartner). Choose pattern based on task structure and expertise division.

Build agents that think before acting using ReAct patterns, Tree-of-Thought exploration, and reflexion loops. These reasoning-first architectures solve the problem of agents making premature or suboptimal decisions by enforcing structured thought processes before action execution. Apply when dealing with complex multi-step problems requiring careful analysis. The value delivered is dramatically improved agent reliability and better decision quality, especially for reasoning-intensive tasks like research and planning.

Create and deploy specialized subagents with isolated contexts for autonomous task execution. This methodology addresses context pollution and uncontrolled task sprawl by giving each agent a focused scope and explicit tool whitelist. Use when building multi-agent systems requiring parallel execution or task specialization. The outcome is cleaner architecture, easier debugging, and the ability to scale work across isolated execution environments without interference.

Build slash commands that accept arguments and deliver fast, single-purpose actions for quick user interactions. Commands solve the problem of context bloat by keeping implementation minimal and lightweight compared to full skills or agents. Apply for frequently-used utilities that need command-line style arguments. The benefit is reduced token consumption and instant user access to commonly repeated actions.

Implement lifecycle automation using pre-tool, post-tool, and session hooks that execute scripts before or after specific events. Hooks solve permission prompts, autoformat code automatically, and enforce security policies without user intervention. Use when you need consistent enforcement of standards across all tool usage or to auto-approve safe operations. The value is reduced friction, consistent quality, and automatic policy application.

Build and configure Model Context Protocol servers to extend Claude's capabilities with custom tools and resources. MCP servers solve the problem of tool sprawl and credential management by providing a standardized interface for integrating external services. Apply when building complex integrations or when you need reusable tool packages. The outcome is modular, shareable tool infrastructure that works across both Claude Code and standalone agents.

Learn the fundamentals of MCP server development in TypeScript or Python with proper tool and resource definitions. This foundation addresses the gap between knowing you want to extend Claude and understanding the protocol mechanics. Use as your starting point before building custom MCP servers. The value is understanding the underlying patterns that enable all MCP integrations, from official servers to custom implementations.

Reference and install 40+ production MCP servers for platforms like GitHub, Slack, PostgreSQL, Figma, Stripe, and more. This catalog solves the discovery problem of "what MCP servers exist for my use case?" Apply when you need external service integration without building from scratch. The benefit is rapidly connecting Claude to your entire tech stack without custom development.

Implement the CLAUDE.md + AGENTS.md documentation pattern that works across both Claude Code and standalone agents. This convention solves the split-brain problem where Claude Code knows about CLAUDE.md but agents read files directly. Apply in every project directory with source code for consistent context discovery. The value is single-source-of-truth documentation accessible to all agent types.

Create reusable procedural knowledge packages that encapsulate workflows, methodologies, and domain expertise. Skills solve the problem of duplicated knowledge by packaging repeatable patterns into discoverable, composable units. Use when you have multi-step workflows needed across projects or when building knowledge libraries. The outcome is a knowledge base that grows more valuable as you add skills and becomes auto-discoverable when needed.

Claude's extended thinking, computer use, prompt caching, and batch API enable sophisticated reasoning and cost optimization. Use extended thinking for complex multi-step problems, computer use for visual workflows, prompt caching to reduce token costs by 90%, and the batch API for non-time-sensitive bulk processing. These features deliver better reasoning quality, visual task automation, and significant cost savings when integrated thoughtfully.

Set up authentication, understand model selection, handle rate limiting, count tokens, and track costs for Claude API integration. This foundation addresses the operational requirements of production LLM systems—you cannot build safely without understanding authentication, quota management, and cost. Use as the prerequisite before any API integration work. The benefit is avoiding common gotchas like auth failures, quota surprises, and unexpected bill spikes.

Integrate Claude into production applications with streaming, async execution, tool use, vision capabilities, and error recovery patterns. This methodology solves the engineering problem of turning API calls into reliable application features that handle edge cases. Apply when building production services that depend on Claude. The outcome is robust integration code that handles errors gracefully, streams responses, and maintains quality under load.

Optimize prompts, cost, performance, and reliability through model selection, prompt engineering, token management, and production patterns. This knowledge addresses the gap between working prototypes and production-ready systems where efficiency matters. Use when transitioning from proof-of-concept to deployed services. The value is dramatically reduced costs, faster responses, and higher quality outputs through systematic optimization.

Master the Messages API for text generation, streaming, vision inputs, PDF processing, and multi-turn conversations. This API is the foundation of all Claude interactions and solves the problem of understanding the core message protocol. Apply whenever building any Claude-powered feature. The benefit is knowing exactly how to structure requests, handle responses, and leverage all modality options available.

Implement tool use and function calling patterns for structured interaction, parallel tool execution, and agentic loops. Tool use solves the problem of moving LLMs from text-only systems to action-oriented agents that can call functions and iterate. Apply when you need the LLM to interact with external systems or when deterministic structure matters. The outcome is agents capable of multi-step task execution with proper error handling.

Apply chain-of-thought prompting with zero-shot, few-shot, and self-consistency techniques to improve LLM reasoning. CoT solves the problem of shallow thinking by forcing step-by-step reasoning. Use for any complex reasoning task where intermediate steps matter. The value is substantially better accuracy on logic, math, and analysis problems through structured reasoning.

Advance beyond basic CoT with Tree-of-Thought exploration, Least-to-Most decomposition, and domain-specific reasoning patterns. These advanced techniques solve the problem of hitting CoT limits with very complex problems. Apply when basic chain-of-thought isn't sufficient or when exploring multiple solution paths adds value. The benefit is handling harder problems through structured exploration and hierarchical decomposition.

Design production function-calling systems with tool routers, parallel execution, agentic loops, and error recovery. This addresses the architectural challenge of scaling from single-tool calls to multi-tool orchestration. Use when building systems with many tools or complex tool interaction logic. The outcome is scalable, reliable tool execution that handles partial failures and tool errors gracefully.

Integrate Google's Gemini API leveraging its 1M token context, native multimodal support, and cost-effectiveness. Gemini solves the problem of processing very long documents, videos, and code repositories that exceed other models' context windows. Apply when you need multimodal processing or have documents exceeding 200K tokens. The value is processing capabilities impossible with smaller context windows and native support for media types.

Understand Gemini models, authentication, text generation, streaming, chat, and safety settings for Google AI integration. This foundation covers the essentials of working with Google's LLM API. Use before diving into advanced Gemini patterns. The benefit is understanding model selection, cost structure, and safety mechanisms specific to Gemini.

Implement function calling, Google Search grounding, and embeddings with Gemini for extended capability and real-time information access. Function calling solves the problem of Gemini-specific tool patterns which differ from OpenAI. Apply when using Gemini in production and needing tool interaction. The outcome is proper integration of Gemini's tool capabilities and real-time information grounding.

Process images, videos, audio, PDFs, and code using Gemini's native multimodal support with caching for cost savings. Multimodal processing solves the problem of handling diverse input types in a single request. Use when working with video understanding, document extraction, or multi-format analysis. The value is unified processing of any media type and context caching that reduces costs by 75%.

Implement safety guardrails through input validation, PII detection, prompt injection detection, and content moderation. Guardrails solve the fundamental safety problem of production LLM systems by adding multiple defense layers. Apply in any user-facing application or when handling sensitive data. The benefit is reduced safety risks and compliance with content policies.

Build complete guardrails pipelines with output validation, hallucination detection, and monitoring for production safety. This goes beyond basics to create comprehensive safety systems that catch issues both before and after LLM execution. Use in regulated industries or when safety is critical. The outcome is comprehensive safety coverage across input validation, processing, output filtering, and monitoring.

Run large language models locally using Ollama for privacy, cost elimination, and offline capability with models like Llama 3 and Mistral. Local LLMs solve the problem of API dependency, data privacy concerns, and per-token costs for high-volume applications. Apply when you have privacy requirements, need offline capability, or want to eliminate API costs. The value is complete data privacy and zero per-inference costs while retaining reasonable performance.

OpenAI API integration provides access to GPT-4 and other models for text generation, embeddings, and multimodal tasks. Apply this when building production conversational AI, content generation, or code analysis applications. The value comes from providing authentication, model selection guidance, streaming responses, structured output handling, and production-ready error handling with retry logic.

OpenAI Assistants API enables stateful AI agents with persistent conversation threads, file search capabilities, and code interpreter access. Use this when you need long-running conversations, knowledge base integration, or automated data analysis without managing tool infrastructure yourself. It delivers value by handling state management, enabling RAG over documents, and allowing Python code execution.

Chat Completions API is the core endpoint for all OpenAI text interactions, supporting streaming, vision analysis, and tool use. Apply it to any application needing text generation, from simple classification to complex multi-turn reasoning. Value derives from low latency, high quality outputs, support for vision understanding, and comprehensive parameter control for output behavior.

Embeddings and Batch API convert text to vector representations for semantic search and enable cost-reduction through non-urgent batch processing. Use embeddings for RAG, similarity search, and clustering; use Batch API for non-time-sensitive workloads like daily summarization jobs. These deliver value through 50% cost savings (batch) and 80%+ recall improvements (semantic search).

Function calling enables LLMs to invoke external tools, APIs, and execute code while maintaining structured reasoning. Apply this when building agents that need to interact with external systems, perform precise calculations, or enforce specific output formats. Value comes from parallel tool execution, reliable schema compliance, and the ability to ground LLM outputs in verifiable actions.

Prompt basics covers core concepts like system messages, few-shot examples, and role-based prompting that form the foundation of effective LLM use. Apply whenever you need consistent LLM behavior across tasks, especially for simple ones like classification or extraction. Value lies in improving accuracy 15-30% through better instruction clarity and format specification without token overhead.

Advanced prompting techniques including chain-of-thought, meta-prompting, prompt chaining, and prompt libraries enable sophisticated multi-step reasoning and template management. Use for complex problems requiring visible reasoning steps, for optimizing prompts via LLM feedback, or for managing production prompt libraries at scale. Value comes from 15-40% accuracy improvements for reasoning tasks and reduced maintenance burden.

Structured output ensures LLMs return data in consistent, machine-readable formats like JSON with schema validation. Apply when integrating LLM outputs directly into applications, APIs, or automated pipelines where format reliability is critical. Value derives from eliminating parsing errors, reducing downstream validation code, and enabling reliable automation of data extraction workflows.

Advanced patterns for production structured output including retry logic, streaming with partial parsing, and service architecture for multi-schema extraction. Use in production systems where output validation failures must be handled gracefully and where batch extraction of diverse data types is needed. Value comes from fault tolerance, 99%+ reliability, and efficient handling of complex extraction scenarios.

Tool use enables LLMs to call external functions, APIs, databases, and execute code as part of their reasoning process. Apply this when building AI agents that need access to real-time data, precise calculations, or system interactions like file management. Value delivered includes dynamic task adaptation, access to live information beyond training data, and the ability to perform verifiable real-world actions.

Agentic RAG combines autonomous agents with retrieval-augmented generation, using agents to iteratively refine queries, grade document relevance, and self-correct outputs. Use when traditional RAG produces hallucinations or fails on complex multi-document queries requiring reasoning across sources. Value comes from 20-30% improvement in answer accuracy and ability to handle ambiguous queries requiring clarification.

AI agent patterns including ReAct, Chain-of-Thought, Plan-Execute, and Tree-of-Thoughts provide structured approaches for autonomous reasoning and task completion. Apply to complex multi-step problems, dynamic environments requiring adaptation, or tasks where visible reasoning is needed for verification. Value comes from 30-50% accuracy improvements for reasoning tasks and clear, auditable decision paths.

AI governance frameworks ensure AI systems are safe, compliant, ethical, and properly audited across model governance, audit trails, responsible AI, and data handling. Apply in regulated industries, enterprises managing multiple AI systems, or organizations needing board-level AI oversight. Value includes regulatory compliance avoiding 7% revenue fines, systematic bias detection preventing PR crises, and centralized model lifecycle management.

Chain-of-Thought prompting breaks complex reasoning into explicit intermediate steps, improving accuracy on math, logic, and multi-step problems. Apply to any task requiring visible reasoning: mathematical word problems, code debugging, complex decisions. Value comes from 15-40% accuracy improvements, ability to detect and correct errors in reasoning, and transparent decision paths for audit.

Chunking strategies optimize how documents are split for RAG systems, balancing semantic coherence with retrieval effectiveness. Apply when RAG accuracy is insufficient or context is wasted on irrelevant text. Value comes from 30-40% retrieval precision improvements, reduced token waste per query (enabling larger context windows), and better answer relevance.

Claude API provides access to Anthropic's Claude models with extended thinking, tool use, vision, and prompt caching for long-context reasoning. Use for tasks requiring deep analysis, long document processing, or complex multi-step problem solving where reasoning transparency matters. Value includes 200K token context (5x GPT-4), 90% caching savings on repeated prompts, and superior reasoning for code/analysis.

Cost optimization strategies including model routing, prompt caching, token reduction, and batching reduce LLM costs 60-90% while maintaining quality. Apply in production systems where cost scales with usage: customer support, content generation, batch processing. Value comes from reducing per-request cost 10-100x depending on strategy combination, enabling profitable AI products.

Decision framework helps select the right approach (prompting vs RAG vs fine-tuning) and model for AI tasks based on capability needs and budget constraints. Apply before building any AI feature to avoid over-engineering or poor cost efficiency. Value lies in 40-60% cost savings through correct approach selection and 15-25% performance gains through proper model-to-task matching.

Embedding generation converts text to dense vectors for semantic search, with guidance on model selection, batching, caching, and optimization. Use for RAG systems, similarity search, clustering, and recommendation systems where semantic understanding matters more than keyword matching. Value comes from 30-50% higher retrieval recall than keyword search, enabling semantic deduplication saving 40% storage.

EU AI Act compliance framework provides risk classification, documentation requirements, and enforcement timeline (full compliance August 2026) for European AI deployment. Apply if you serve EU markets or need to manage regulatory risk. Value comes from avoiding 3-7% revenue fines, systematic compliance processes, and competitive advantage in regulated AI markets.

LoRA and variants (QLoRA, DoRA) enable efficient fine-tuning using adapter layers instead of full model updates, reducing memory 10-20x. Use when you need model customization but lack enterprise hardware; enables 70B models on consumer GPUs. Value comes from 90-95% of full fine-tuning quality at 10-15% of GPU memory cost, enabling rapid experimentation.

LLM fine-tuning decision framework and technique comparison helps determine whether to fine-tune, when to use alternatives like RAG, and which framework to choose. Apply before any fine-tuning project to validate necessity and select optimal approach. Value comes from avoiding expensive fine-tuning when cheaper solutions exist, and 40-60% faster training through framework selection.

Google Gemini API provides multimodal AI capabilities with native support for images, video, audio, and PDFs up to 2M token context. Use it when you need advanced vision understanding, real-time voice processing, or highly cost-effective inference on complex tasks. Delivers superior performance on medical imaging and multimodal tasks while maintaining low latency and offering context caching for 75% cost savings.

GraphRAG combines knowledge graphs with vector retrieval to answer questions requiring entity relationships and multi-hop reasoning, excelling where standard RAG struggles. Deploy it when questions need to understand how entities connect, find global themes across documents, or reason through multiple relationships. Improves multi-hop accuracy by 62% and hallucination reduction by 40% compared to pure vector search.

Hybrid search combines keyword matching (BM25) with semantic vector search to maximize retrieval quality by capturing both exact terms and conceptual meaning. Use it for technical documentation, legal search, and domain-specific Q&A where queries mix codes, names, and natural language. Achieves 87% relevant document precision in top 10, a 42% improvement over pure vector search.

AI image generation creates visuals from text descriptions using models like DALL-E 3, Stable Diffusion, or Flux for marketing, design, and prototyping. Use it for on-brand asset generation, rapid UI mockups, or creative exploration where consistency and customization matter. Best models handle text rendering, anatomy, and prompt adherence, costing $0.014-0.12 per image depending on quality.

LangChain orchestrates multi-step AI workflows with composable chains and agentic patterns using LCEL syntax and LangGraph for state management. Choose it for building production agents with tool calling, RAG pipelines, and multi-agent systems when you need explicit control flow and observability. Supports streaming, retry logic, and works with any LLM provider via a unified interface.

LlamaIndex specializes in building document-centric RAG and agentic systems with 1500+ data connectors and multiple index types from vector stores to knowledge graphs. Adopt it for enterprise search, contract analysis, or research assistants when you have rich, varied data sources and need sophisticated document parsing and indexing. Superior to LangChain for RAG specifically, offering LlamaParse for complex PDFs.

This framework guides you to choose between prompting, RAG, fine-tuning, and hybrid approaches based on requirements, ROI, and constraints. Use it when deciding architecture for a new project: prompt engineering is free and immediate, RAG handles dynamic data, fine-tuning adds domain expertise but requires retraining. Provides cost and complexity analysis to match solution to problem.

LLM observability tracks token usage, cost, output quality, hallucination rates, and reasoning steps across your AI application stack. Implement it to monitor production systems, debug prompt performance, track costs by user/team, and evaluate output relevance beyond traditional APM metrics. Platforms like Langfuse, LangSmith, and Helicone provide tracing, cost analytics, and LLM-as-judge evaluation.

A complete observability infrastructure combines tracing (Langfuse), cost analytics (Helicone), quality evaluation (Phoenix), and alerting (Prometheus) with OpenTelemetry standards for LLM applications. Build it to handle non-deterministic outputs, monitor multi-turn conversations, track agent reasoning, and prevent cost overruns. 2026 trend: OTEL-native SDKs consolidate vendor fragmentation.

Model evaluation systematically measures LLM performance across quality, latency, cost, and safety using benchmarks like MMLU, HumanEval, and LLM-as-judge scoring. Implement it before production deployment to compare models, tune prompts, and monitor degradation. Key metrics: accuracy, faithfulness, relevance, and hallucination rate with automated assessment on standard benchmarks.

Design patterns for multi-agent systems enable specialized agents to collaborate through supervisor routing, hierarchical decomposition, sequential pipelines, or peer-to-peer coordination. Choose pattern based on problem structure: supervisor for request routing, hierarchical for complex goal breakdown, sequential for deterministic workflows, peer-to-peer for resilience. Gartner reports 1,445% surge in enterprise multi-agent interest.

Multi-agent systems coordinate multiple LLM agents to solve complex tasks through LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, or OpenAI Swarm frameworks. Select framework by control needs: LangGraph for explicit state management, CrewAI for role-based teams, AutoGen for conversational collaboration. Requires attention to state management, observability, guardrails, and timeout handling in production.

OpenAI APIs provide chat completions, vision, function calling, embeddings, audio, and image generation with GPT-4o as the flagship model. Use the newer Responses API for stateful conversations and reasoning models (o1, o3); migrate from Chat Completions if building new applications. Implement structured outputs for reliable JSON, prompt caching for repeated prefixes, and batch API for 50% cost savings.

RAG grounds LLM responses in external knowledge by retrieving relevant documents at query time, enabling current information, source citations, and reduced hallucinations. Deploy it when information changes frequently, needs attribution, exceeds context limits, or when cost matters (cheaper than fine-tuning with no retraining needed). Core components: chunking, embedding, vector store, reranking, synthesis.

RAG evaluation measures retrieval quality (precision, recall, MRR, NDCG) and generation quality (faithfulness, relevance, hallucination rate) using the RAGAS framework or LLM-as-judge. Test both dimensions: retrieval recall predicts available information, generation metrics measure answer accuracy. Implement continuous evaluation to catch pipeline degradation and guide optimization of chunking, embedding models, or rerankers.

RAG pipeline design covers architecture tiers (naive to agentic), component selection (embeddings, vector DB, chunking strategy), and production deployment patterns. Start with naive RAG for simple Q&A, advance to modular RAG for multiple sources, adopt agentic RAG for complex reasoning. Key decisions: Qdrant for self-hosted, Pinecone for managed, semantic chunking for quality, hybrid search for precision.

Reasoning-first models (OpenAI o3, Claude with Extended Thinking, DeepSeek R1) allocate test-time compute to internal reasoning before visible output, achieving breakthrough performance on complex math, code, and analysis. Use them for problems with multiple solution paths, edge cases, or requiring verification when standard models perform poorly. Costs more per token but delivers o3 at 96.7% MATH-500 vs GPT-4o at 76.6%.

Reranking uses cross-encoders to refine initial retrieval results with more sophisticated relevance scoring, improving precision while accepting higher latency. Add it to production RAG when initial retrieval quality is weak or you need balanced BM25+vector fusion; skip for latency-critical applications under 100ms. Improves RAG accuracy by 20-35% with models like Jina v3, Cohere Rerank, or BGE.

Speech-to-text converts audio to text with models like OpenAI Whisper (99+ languages, $0.006/min) or specialized services like AssemblyAI for diarization. Use it for meeting transcription, podcast processing, voice command recognition, or accessibility when you need accurate multi-language support and flexible latency (batch or real-time). Local deployment via Whisper for privacy or high-volume cost savings.

Structured output forces LLMs to return JSON-formatted responses that comply with schemas, enabling reliable parsing for APIs and automated workflows. Use native structured output (OpenAI, Gemini) when available for guaranteed schema compliance; fall back to JSON mode or tool calling for other providers. Define schemas with Pydantic for type safety, validation, and IDE support.

Text-to-speech converts written text into natural-sounding audio through AI-powered voice synthesis. Use this when you need to generate spoken content for accessibility, voiceovers, podcasts, or interactive applications. Modern TTS providers like ElevenLabs and OpenAI offer human-quality voices with fine-grained control over tone, speed, and emotion, enabling you to create professional audio content at scale with minimal production overhead.

Tool use and function calling enable LLMs to interact with external systems by generating structured requests that your code then executes, forming the foundation of agentic AI systems. Use this pattern when you need LLMs to make API calls, query databases, or trigger workflows rather than just generate text. This capability transforms models from text generators into action-taking agents that can solve complex multi-step problems by combining reasoning with real-world operations.

Vector databases are specialized stores optimized for semantic search by finding similar items based on embedding distance rather than exact matches. Use this to understand which database best fits your use case, from Qdrant's production RAG capabilities to Pinecone's serverless simplicity to pgvector's SQL integration. Choosing the right database at the start prevents costly migrations later and directly impacts your application's search quality, latency, and scaling characteristics.

Voice agents provide real-time conversational AI over telephony and WebRTC with latency under 1 second for natural turn-taking and speaker presence. Use this to build customer service bots, sales agents, or interactive voice assistants that handle back-and-forth dialogue with speech-to-text, reasoning, and text-to-speech in a continuous loop. Platforms like Retell AI and ElevenLabs handle the infrastructure complexity, letting you focus on conversation logic and business outcomes.

Cost reduction strategies for LLM APIs include response caching, prompt optimization, model routing, and batching to reduce token usage and API costs while maintaining quality. Use this when your LLM costs are becoming a bottleneck or when scaling to production volume makes unit economics critical. Well-executed cost optimization can reduce your monthly bill by 20-50% while often improving latency and reliability through smarter routing and caching.

Evaluation benchmarks provide standardized test suites for comparing models, measuring performance over time, and monitoring production quality through automated metrics and LLM judges. Use this to systematically track whether your LLM system is improving or degrading as you iterate, and to compare vendors or model versions before switching. Regular benchmarking prevents silent quality regressions and gives you data-driven confidence in upgrades or architecture changes.

The evaluation framework provides core abstractions for testing LLM outputs against ground truth, using automated metrics, and leveraging LLM-as-judge for complex tasks without clear metrics. Use this to build test harnesses that validate your models work before shipping to production and to detect regressions. A good evaluation framework turns subjective quality assessment into reproducible, quantifiable measurement.

Evaluation metrics measure LLM performance across accuracy, quality, latency, cost, safety, and reliability dimensions with task-specific measures for classification, QA, summarization, and code.
